title: AI Has a Memory Problem
gist: CSIS's Shruti Sharma argues memory chips, not just GPUs, are becoming the binding constraint on AI deployment: SK Hynix has sold out its 2026 HBM production, OpenAI's Stargate has reportedly locked up roughly 40% of global DRAM output, and industry forecasts see shortages persisting through at least 2027 as data centers claim about 70% of world memory supply.

insights.claim: Global memory chip sales rose nearly 79% in 2024 to $165 billion and are projected to exceed $223 billion in 2025, driven by AI data-center demand.
insights.speaker: Shruti Sharma
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of the AI-driven memory supercycle -- a clean read-through to Samsung/SK Hynix/Micron revenue and pricing power in 2025-2026.
insights.quote: Global memory sales increased nearly 79 percent in 2024 to $165 billion and are projected to exceed $223 billion in 2025
insights.locator: The concerns come amid a period of rapid growth

insights.claim: The author argues memory availability, not just advanced logic/GPU capacity, is becoming an equally important constraint on AI deployment and broader industrial competitiveness -- a factor that has received far less policy attention than accelerator chips.
insights.speaker: Shruti Sharma
insights.so_what: Reframes the AI-hardware bottleneck debate: investors and policymakers focused solely on logic/GPU supply may be underweighting memory as an equally binding constraint.
insights.quote: memory availability may become an equally important determinant of AI deployment and broader industrial competitiveness
insights.locator: recent developments suggest that memory availability

insights.claim: Meta, Microsoft, Amazon, and Alphabet are projected to roughly triple their combined annual AI data-center spending from $217 billion in 2024 to about $650 billion by 2026.
insights.speaker: Shruti Sharma
insights.so_what: Sizes the hyperscaler capex wave driving memory demand -- a leading indicator for memory-supplier order books and capex guidance through 2026.
insights.locator: Between 2024 and 2026, Meta, Microsoft, Amazon, and Alphabet

insights.claim: OpenAI reportedly secured 2025 agreements for up to 900,000 DRAM wafers per month for its Stargate project -- equivalent to roughly 40% of global DRAM output -- effectively cornering a large share of world memory supply for one company's infrastructure buildout.
insights.speaker: Shruti Sharma
insights.so_what: A single-customer demand shock of this size is a structural reason to expect persistent memory shortages and price power for suppliers, and a competitive disadvantage for any AI lab without similar long-term supply locks.
insights.quote: OpenAI reportedly secured agreements in 2025 for up to 900,000 DRAM wafers per month for its Stargate project, equivalent to roughly 40 percent of global DRAM output.

insights.claim: SK Hynix, the leading producer of high-bandwidth memory (HBM), has sold out its entire 2026 production slate and is no longer accepting new orders for major memory products, indicating a physical supply constraint rather than just a price increase.
insights.speaker: Shruti Sharma
insights.so_what: A hard capacity ceiling for SK Hynix through 2026 -- bullish for pricing/margins at incumbent HBM makers and a direct constraint on any AI infrastructure buildout that assumed spot availability.
insights.quote: sold out its entire 2026 production slate and is no longer accepting new orders for major memory products
insights.locator: SK Hynix—the leading producer of high-bandwidth memory

insights.claim: Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron together control more than 90% of global DRAM production and are all shifting output mix toward HBM to meet hyperscaler and AI-firm demand.
insights.speaker: Shruti Sharma
insights.so_what: Defines the three-firm oligopoly that will capture the pricing upside of the memory shortage -- the direct equity/supply-chain plays on the AI memory bottleneck.
insights.locator: Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron—which account for more than 90 percent

insights.claim: Data-center applications are projected to consume roughly 70% of worldwide memory output in 2026, crowding out supply for other memory-intensive industries like telecom, automotive, and medical devices.
insights.speaker: Shruti Sharma
insights.so_what: Signals margin/cost pressure ahead for non-AI, memory-intensive industries (telecom equipment, auto electronics, medical devices) as AI captures the marginal supply.
insights.locator: which are projected to consume roughly 70 percent

insights.claim: New memory fabrication facilities cost $15-20 billion and take years to bring online, so industry forecasts suggest the current memory shortage could persist through at least 2027, even as Samsung, SK Hynix, Micron, and emerging Chinese producers invest heavily in new capacity.
insights.speaker: Shruti Sharma
insights.so_what: Sets a multi-year floor under memory prices/margins -- the shortage is a supply-elasticity problem, not a transient spike, supporting a longer investment horizon on memory suppliers.
insights.quote: shortages could persist through at least 2027 and potentially beyond
insights.locator: New memory fabrication facilities require investments

insights.claim: In June 2026, a coalition of U.S. trade associations representing telecom, auto, medical device, and retail industries formally urged the Trump administration to address memory chip shortages that are raising costs for downstream industries.
insights.speaker: Shruti Sharma
insights.so_what: A live policy trigger -- watch for possible federal intervention (subsidies, export/allocation rules) in the memory market, which would reshape supplier economics.
insights.quote: urged the Trump administration to address emerging shortages in the memory chip market
insights.locator: In June 2026, a coalition of U.S. trade associations
